title: Combating Global Corruption Act of 2025
stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subs
plain_summary: This bill requires the US government to create a yearly report that ranks countries around the world based on how well they fight corruption in their governments. Countries get put into three groups, with tier one being the best at fighting corruption and tier three being the worst. For countries in tiers two and three, the US must assign special workers at American embassies to help those countries fight corruption better. For the worst countries in tier three, the US government must look for corrupt people and consider punishing them with sanctions that can include freezing their money or banning them from entering the United States.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Foreign Affairs, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such provi
subjects: Government ethics and transparency, public corruption
Sovereignty, recognition, national governance and status

sections.title: Section 1. Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Combating Global Corruption Act of twenty twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2. Definitions
sections.text: In this law, these terms mean the following things. A corrupt actor is any foreign person or organization that is a government official or government group responsible for corruption or helping with corruption. It also includes any company where such a person or group owns a large part and that company is responsible for corruption or helps with corruption. Corruption means the illegal use of trusted public power for personal benefit, including through bribery, giving jobs to family members, fraud, or stealing money. Significant corruption means corruption done by high level government officials that illegally changes major decisions like policies or how resources are used or other basic government functions. It also involves large scale government activities that affect the economy or society in big ways.

sections.title: Section 3. Publication of Tiered Ranking List
sections.text: The Secretary of State must publish a ranking list of all foreign countries every year on a website that everyone can access. A country gets ranked as tier one if its government is following the minimum standards explained in section four. A country gets ranked as tier two if its government is trying to follow the minimum standards explained in section four but is not doing well enough to be ranked as tier one. A country gets ranked as tier three if its government is making very little effort or no effort to follow the minimum standards explained in section four.

sections.title: Section 4. Minimum Standards for the Elimination of Corruption and Assessment of Efforts to Combat Corruption
sections.text: A country's government is following the minimum standards to eliminate corruption if it has created and put into action laws and established government structures, policies, and practices that ban and generally prevent corruption including significant corruption. The government must also enforce these laws by punishing anyone who is found through a fair court process to have broken these laws. The government must set punishments for significant corruption that are as serious as punishments for other serious crimes. The government must also be making serious and ongoing efforts to address corruption including through prevention. When deciding if a government is making serious and ongoing efforts to address corruption, the Secretary of State will consider factors like whether the country has made corruption illegal, investigates and prosecutes corrupt acts, and convicts and sentences people responsible for these acts including putting convicted people in prison when appropriate. The Secretary will also consider whether the government strongly investigates, prosecutes, convicts, and sentences public officials who participate in or help with corruption, including citizens of that country who are working in foreign military assignments, trade delegations abroad, or similar missions who engage in or help with significant corruption. Other factors include whether the government has taken steps to prevent corruption such as informing and educating the public including potential victims about what causes corruption and what happens because of it. The Secretary will consider whether the government has taken steps to stop government officials from participating in, helping with, or allowing corruption including investigating, prosecuting, and convicting such officials. The Secretary will look at how much the country provides access or makes adequate resources available to civil society organizations and other institutions to combat corruption including reporting, investigating, and monitoring. The Secretary will consider whether an independent court system or judicial body in the country is responsible for and effectively able to decide corruption cases fairly based on facts and according to the law without any improper restrictions, influences, payments, pressures, threats, or interference either direct or indirect. The Secretary will consider whether the government helps with international investigations of corruption networks that cross country borders and other cooperative efforts to combat significant corruption including cooperating with other countries' governments to send corrupt actors back to face justice. The Secretary will look at whether the government recognizes the rights of corruption victims, ensures their access to justice, and takes steps to prevent victims from being further victimized or persecuted by corrupt actors, government officials, or others. The Secretary will consider whether the government protects corruption victims or whistleblowers (people who report wrongdoing) from revenge because these people helped expose corruption and does not treat such people unfairly in other ways. The Secretary will look at whether the government is willing and able to recover and when appropriate return the money gained from corruption. The Secretary will consider whether the government is taking steps to put in place financial transparency measures in line with Financial Action Task Force recommendations including due diligence and beneficial ownership transparency requirements (rules that require showing who really owns companies). The Secretary will look at whether the government is helping corruption happen in other countries in connection with government directed investment, loans, or grants for major infrastructure or other initiatives. The Secretary will also consider any other information about corruption that the Secretary thinks is appropriate. When deciding if a government is making serious and ongoing efforts to address corruption, the Secretary of State will consider how well the country's government follows these international agreements as relevant. These include the Inter American Convention against Corruption of the Organization of American States done at Caracas March twenty nine nineteen ninety six. The Convention on Combating Bribery of Foreign Public Officials in International Business Transactions of the Organisation of Economic Co operation and Development done at Paris December twenty one nineteen ninety seven commonly called the Anti Bribery Convention. The United Nations Convention against Transnational Organized Crime done at New York November fifteen two thousand. The United Nations Convention against Corruption done at New York October thirty one two thousand three. The Secretary may also consider other treaties, agreements, and international standards that the Secretary thinks are appropriate.

sections.title: Section 5. Imposition of Sanctions Under Global Magnitsky Human Rights Accountability Act
sections.text: The Secretary of State working with the Secretary of the Treasury should evaluate whether there are foreign people engaged in significant corruption for the purpose of possibly putting sanctions under the Global Magnitsky Human Rights Accountability Act (a law that allows punishment of people who violate human rights) on them in all countries identified as tier three countries under section three. No later than one hundred eighty days after publishing the list required by section three and every year after that, the Secretary of State must submit to the committees listed in this section a report. The report must include a list of foreign people that the President put sanctions on because of the evaluation described above. The report must include the dates when these sanctions were put in place. The report must include the reasons for putting these sanctions in place. Each report must be submitted in unclassified form but may include a classified section. The Secretary of State working with the Secretary of the Treasury may provide a briefing to the specified committees instead of submitting a written report if doing so would better serve existing United States anti corruption efforts or the national interests of the United States. The specified committees are the Committee on Foreign Relations, the Committee on Appropriations, the Committee on Banking Housing and Urban Affairs, and the Committee on the Judiciary of the Senate. They also include the Committee on Foreign Affairs, the Committee on Appropriations, the Committee on Financial Services, and the Committee on the Judiciary of the House of Representatives.

sections.title: Section 6. Designation of Embassy Anti-Corruption Points of Contact
sections.text: The Secretary of State must every year choose an anti corruption point of contact at the United States diplomatic post (embassy or consulate) to each country identified as tier two or tier three under section three or which the Secretary otherwise decides needs such a point of contact. The point of contact will be the chief of mission (the person in charge of the embassy or consulate) or someone the chief of mission chooses. Each anti corruption point of contact chosen under this section will be responsible for improving coordination and promoting the use of a whole of government approach among the relevant Federal departments and agencies working to promote good governance in foreign countries. They will also work to enhance the ability of such countries to combat public corruption and to develop and put in place corruption risk assessment tools and strategies to reduce risk. The Secretary of State must put in place appropriate training for anti corruption points of contact chosen under this section.
